title: The chart's silences — flows known to history that the flow matrix does not (or cannot) quantify
note: PLAN-3 §1 rule 5: a known trade is never silently zero, and a silence we cannot answer is recorded HERE, with its reason and our chosen treatment, instead of being dropped. treatment=asserted means the silence was answered — `pointer` names the system carrying our stated estimate; gestured means acknowledged, and a candidate for ambient content (S3) without volume claims; excluded means out of scope, with the reason on record. Basin authoring (R3) appends to this register; S3 renders it as a reference page.

silences.note: The ships rankings count foreign-going clearances (R1 decision), so Europe's single largest generator of ship movements is invisible to them. Answered: the flow matrix carries a reconstructed collier system (~150 voyages/yr in the 1550s → ~4,000–6,500/yr by 1810).

Coasting dwarfed foreign-going traffic in vessel numbers everywhere — grain hoys, wine barks, fish carriers, timber floats. No source counts it; no honest range exists at world scale. Acknowledged as the sea's ambient metabolism; candidate for ambient rendering (S3) rather than a volume claim.

Hundreds of herring busses at sea every season — among the largest fleets afloat, but grounds-traffic rather than port-to-port trade, so it fits no lane. Candidate ambient feature; the fishery's SIZE is well documented even though it has no origin→destination shape.

The anchoring series is itself distorted: under-declaration, wartime neutral re-flagging, and the Swedish exemption (1645–1710) all bias the registers the 'counted' systems rest on. Carried as a caveat on evidence=counted rather than as a flow — the register records that even the archive's firmest ground is soft.
Boats below burden thresholds — wherries, prams, coasters under ~20 tons — were never registered anywhere. Below the simulation's vessel scale; excluded knowingly.

silences.scope: Caribbean (the free ports and the asiento's shadow)
silences.note: Silent BY EVASION — unrecorded because recording was the danger. Answered at R3: the atlantic basin carries an asserted system (±60% bounds around free-port scholarship's central guesses; St Eustatius alone logged 3,000+ ship calls at its 1770s peak, most of them the traffic everyone else's customs never saw).

The great structural silence of the European record: the Nanyang junk trade generated no western toll series, yet the Nagasaki registers — where a state DID count — show Chinese hulls outnumbering Dutch ten to one. Answered at R3 by reconstruction (Blussé, Reid, Sugihara), not assertion: the scholarship is strong enough to class it reconstructed.

The coastal junk metabolism of the world's largest economy — the Shanghai shachuan grain fleet alone counted in the thousands. Answered at R3 (asserted, ±60%) on the same consistency grounds as the English colliers: if Newcastle's coasting is in, Tianjin's is.

Resolved at R3 (decision 4): the Black Sea trade at Kaffa and the Indian Ocean slave trades (see also indian-ocean-slave-trades) are quantified as sober-framed systems rather than left as silences — charter rule 6's framing block is validator-enforced on both. Sim-side representation waits for S2's generalization of the Middle-Passage invariant.

Canoe-house, lagoon, and coastal commerce along the Gold Coast, the Bight, and the Swahili littoral — real, substantial, organized (the Efik houses ran it on their own terms), and with no defensible voyage unit at the sim's vessel scale. GESTURED (R3 decision): acknowledged here, candidate for ambient representation, no volume claim fabricated.

Polynesian and Micronesian seafaring continued throughout this era, entirely outside every European series — no voyage-count basis exists at all. GESTURED (R3 decision): recorded so the chart's Pacific emptiness reads as the archive's silence, not the ocean's.

The port-lifecycle pass (2026-07-14) briefly EXCLUDED the cod flow outside Louisbourg's life (1713–58) — the St John's/Placentia/Canso fishery, among the largest voyage-generators of the whole era. RESOLVED the same day by era-naming: the node sails the full era and reads ST JOHN'S outside the fortress years (ports.json eraNames), so the fishery is charted under the honest name of its time. St John's proper (~500 km east) remains a promotion-queue refinement.

Smeerenburg the STATION died c.1660, but the Dutch and German fishery sailed on offshore — ice-edge whaling with no port call at all (the counted Dutch series continues to ~1800). The lifecycle pass ends the station's lanes at 1660; the offshore fishery has no node to visit by its nature. EXCLUDED with this record; a 'grounds' node is a possible future exception.

Kaffa's lanes end with the Russian annexation of 1783 — but the grain kept flowing to Istanbul, increasingly from new Russian ports (Kherson 1778, Odessa 1794). The lifecycle pass would otherwise sail it to a port whose trade, as represented here, had ended. EXCLUDED, not zeroed: Odessa is a natural promotion-queue candidate for the late-era Black Sea.

Between the VOC's end and the 1817 resumption the Dutch-Japan trade did not stop, but it stopped being Dutch-carried: with the Republic gone (management dissolved 1796, charter lapsed 1799) and the British blockading, ~13 arrivals 1797-1809 came on CHARTERED AMERICAN HULLS flying Dutch colours out of Batavia (Eliza, Franklin, Massachusetts, Margaret, Rebecca, America, Mount Vernon...). Then it stopped entirely: no ship reached Dejima for three years from 1810 - the Nagasaki kaisho issued the factory's food monthly by shogunal order - and 1815-16 were blank too; eight shipless years in all (1804, 1805, 1808, 1810-12, 1815-16). In 1813-14 the only ships were British-sent under Raffles, flying the Dutch flag, which opperhoofd Hendrik Doeff refused to hand the factory over to. The flow model carries one flag per lane and cannot express 'American hull, Dutch colours, no ship at all this year', so the period is gestured here rather than smoothed into either voc-japan (which correctly ends 1795) or dutch-japan (which correctly begins 1817). Verified 2026-07-18.

silences.scope: The Mediterranean corsair economy and its captives, both directions, c.1530-1830
silences.note: Corsairing was predation, not a trade lane, so it cannot be a flow system: no cargo moved between two termini on a schedule. It is recorded here instead. Robert Davis estimated roughly 850,000 Europeans taken by North African corsairs between 1580 and 1680, and 1 to 1.25 million between 1530 and 1780; those figures are genuinely contested - Earle and Toledano among others argue the extrapolation from ransom and population records overstates the totals - and they are carried here WITH that contestation rather than as a settled number. The register is deliberately BIDIRECTIONAL: Muslim and North African captives were held in Christian hands in the same centuries, at Malta, in the bagni of Livorno and Genoa, and at the oar in the galley fleets of every Mediterranean power. Recording only one direction would be both dishonest and an invitation to misuse. Neither direction is a value tier, a cargo, or a reward. Entered 2026-07-18.

silences.scope: Saharan caravan goods re-exported by sea from the Barbary coast, whole era
silences.note: Tripoli, and to a lesser degree Tunis and Benghazi, were the sea ends of Saharan caravan routes: gold, ivory, hides, ostrich feathers, and enslaved people crossed the desert and some part of that traffic went onward by ship. The maritime re-export leg has no series - the caravan record and the port record were never joined - so barbary-regency-exports carries only the goods it can defend and this share is gestured rather than counted into it. The coerced portion of the caravan traffic is not quantified here and is not made a cargo; it is named because leaving it out would be a silent zero on the trade the Saharan routes are best documented for. Entered 2026-07-18.

silences.scope: Guayaquil cacao to New Spain under the ban, 1631-1774
silences.note: Cacao moved from Guayaquil to Mexico for the whole century and a half that the crown forbade it. This was not a tolerated exception to the 1631 ban - it was contraband, and the correction matters, because modelling it as a legal flow beginning in 1631 would launder a smuggling trade into a licensed one and would also double the post-1774 series. The guayaquil-cacao system therefore begins at the 1774 reopening and the earlier traffic is asserted here: real, continuous, and unquantified, because a trade conducted to avoid the record did not leave one. Entered 2026-07-18.

Beneath the Armada and the licensed runs there was a continuous small-craft coasting trade along the Pacific littoral - Paita, Arica, Pisco, Realejo, the intermediate roads - carrying wine, wheat, timber, and people between colonial ports. Almost none of it was registered. Paita in particular functioned as a waystage rather than a terminus and is a waypoint here, not a node. pacific-colonial-spanish counts the trunk it can defend; this is the traffic underneath it, gestured. Entered 2026-07-18.

silences.scope: Bantam & Lampung (pre-VOC Muslim & Chinese-junk pepper carriage)
silences.note: The independent Muslim and Chinese-junk pepper carriage out of Bantam and Lampung goes dark in the record precisely because the VOC monopoly forced that trade to Batavia and suppressed independent carriage - Lampung ceded 12 March 1682, the monopoly letter 22 August 1682, the conquest 1682-84. A DISPLACEMENT, not a disappearance: the counted post-1684 Batavia series is the same pepper re-routed under monopoly, and reading the two as a fall and a rise would invent a collapse that did not happen. Answered by the bantam-pepper system. Precedent chain: Palembang 1642, Makassar 1669, Banten 1682, Jambi 1683. Entered 2026-07-18.

silences.scope: Persia to the Levant, Mediterranean, India, Russia, and Manila (multi-basin, Armenian account)
silences.note: The Julfan Armenian carriage (New Julfa, Isfahan, 1605; floruit c.1600-1750, broken by the 1722 Afghan sack) moved Persian raw silk, gems, and indigo across the whole Eurasian arc - to Aleppo, Smyrna, Venice, Livorno, Marseille, Amsterdam; to Surat, Madras, Manila; and north via Astrakhan into Russia - almost entirely on OTHER flags' bottoms and by overland caravan. A flow matrix keyed to lanes and flags cannot hold a trade whose defining feature was that it sailed under everyone else's colours; its shadow is already inside greek-ottoman-coasting, persian-gulf-trade, and country-trade-west. Even its one counted stratum, the Russia-transit silk series (12,032 kg/yr in 1676-85 rising to 79,673 in 1743-47, 60-70% Armenian), is volume rather than voyages and moved overland. The single leg where Julfans were isolable shipping principals IS mapped, as armenian-madras-manila. Entered 2026-07-18.

Bolts's Imperial Asiatic Company of Trieste planted two short-lived stations - Nancowery in the Nicobars (1778-1785) and Delagoa Bay (1777-1781, expelled by a Portuguese frigate out of Goa). Attempted colonial footholds that FAILED, carrying negligible commercial flow; their significance is political rather than a trade the chart omits. The company's real traffic folds into minor-company-arterials. Recorded so the absence of a Nicobar dot is a stated decision rather than an oversight. Entered 2026-07-18.
